text: {"claims":[{"statement":"Including eggs in a diet helped to maintain lean body mass, probably due to their high protein content.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"including eggs in a diet helped high protein content.","population":"","intervention":"including eggs in a diet","comparator":"","outcome":"maintain lean body months.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"egg consumption was associated with significantly greater choline intakes and greater recumbent length","entailment":"The quote links egg consumption to higher choline intake and longer recumbent length, indicating an association.","population":"infants aged 6–24 months (n=2690)","intervention":"egg consumption","comparator":"","outcome":"choline intake; recumbent length","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"significantly greater","modality":"associational"},{"statement":"Consuming 7–14 eggs per week within a varied and balanced diet could be beneficial for most of the population in terms of increasing nutrient density and providing high‑quality protein which can protect lean body mass and improve the satiating quality of meals.","claimType":"definitional","quote":"7–14 eggs/week within a varied and balanced diet could be beneficial for most of the population in terms of increasing nutrient density and providing high-quality protein which can protect lean body mass and improve the satiating quality of meals","entailment":"The sentence defines a suggested intake range and the expected benefits, framing it as a protection; satiety","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"7–14 eggs/week","modality":"conditional"},{"statement":"Eggs have a lower environmental impact than other animal that of other animal proteins, asserting a lower impact.","population":"","intervention":"eggs","comparator":"other animal proteins","outcome":"environmental impact","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"descriptive"},{"statement":"Early introduction of allergenic foods may lower risk of food allergy in
